## Research Plan — pl_002

**Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845 Ireland, d. 1908 Schuylkill County, PA)?

**Starting point:** Patrick was found age 5 in the household of Thomas Flynn (head, b. ~1818) in the 1850 Schuylkill County census. Thomas is the leading candidate father under hypothesis h_001, but census co-enumeration is only indirect evidence. The mother is unnamed in any record found so far.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Where / When | Repository | Why it matters |
|---|-----|-------------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | pli_004 | **1860 Census** | Schuylkill Co., PA · 1860 | FamilySearch (free, indexed) | Patrick (~15) likely still in Thomas's household; reveals wife's first name for the first time |
| 2 | pli_005 | **1880 Census** | Schuylkill Co., PA · 1880 | FamilySearch (free, indexed) | 1880 **names relationship to head** — if Patrick appears as Thomas's "son," that is the strongest census evidence for parentage |
| 3 | pli_006 | **Patrick's death certificate** | Schuylkill Co., PA · 1908 | FamilySearch (indexed) | PA civil registration started 1906; cert includes father's name and mother's maiden name (derivative, but directs all later searches) |
| 4 | pli_007 | **Probate — Thomas Flynn** | Schuylkill Co. · 1860–1900 | FamilySearch (collection 1417683 + browse image group **007936749**) | Will or admin naming Patrick as heir = **direct** parentage evidence; image group is 0% indexed, requires browse |
| 5 | pli_008 | **Thomas Flynn × wife marriage record** | Schuylkill Co. · 1835–1850 | FamilySearch (browse image group **007936751**) | **Dedicated item per planning standards** — sole likely source for the mother's maiden name; marriage date relative to Patrick's birth confirms the couple before 1845 |
| 6 | pli_009 | **Patrick's baptism** | Schuylkill Co. · 1845–1855 | FamilySearch (collection 1401638 + image group **007936751**) | A baptism entry naming "Thomas Flynn and [wife]" as parents would be direct, primary evidence — highest-quality result possible |
| 7 | pli_010 | **Immigration (ship manifests)** | Ports: Philadelphia / NY / Boston · 1845–1849 | FamilySearch (indexed) | Famine-era manifest listing Thomas + wife + infant Patrick confirms the family unit before Pennsylvania settlement; may name the mother |
| 8 | pli_011 | **Naturalization — Thomas Flynn** | Schuylkill Co. · 1849–1875 | FamilySearch (browse image group **007936750**, 0% indexed) | Declaration/petition may name wife and children; witnesses are FAN-cluster leads for future searches |

---

### Key notes

- **Three records require browsing unindexed image groups** (probate 007936749, church 007936751, naturalization 007936750) — standard keyword searches will miss them.
- **Mother's maiden name** is the central gap: items 5 (marriage), 6 (baptism), and 8 (naturalization) are the three paths to it.
- **No fallback items needed yet** — each item targets a distinct source type. If items 1–3 all yield nothing, that itself is significant evidence to note.
- **FAN context:** The wiki notes Schuylkill County's Irish coal-mining community was close-knit; if Thomas Flynn doesn't appear directly, neighbors in the same dwelling block in 1850 may be relatives worth tracking.

---

**Estimated scope:** 8 searches (3 indexed, 3 browse-only, 1 indexed with browse fallback, 1 indexed immigration).

**Suggested next step:** Start with item 1 — the 1860 Schuylkill County census on FamilySearch. Would you like me to execute the plan?

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn (~15 in 1860) was likely still living in Thomas Flynn's household. The 1860 census would confirm Thomas's wife's given name and family composition at a time when Patrick was old enough to be reliably recorded. Finding Patrick co-enumerated with Thomas and wife again, a decade after 1850, strengthens the parent-child hypothesis and reveals the mother's first name, which is absent from the 1850 enumeration. Free and indexed on FamilySearch; highest probability for direct confirmation of continued co-residence.

The 1880 census introduced a relationship-to-head column, making it the most diagnostically powerful census for parentage. Thomas Flynn (~62 in 1880) may still be alive; if Patrick (~35) appears in Thomas's household with a stated relationship of 'son,' that is the strongest census evidence for parentage obtainable. Even if Patrick has his own household by then, Thomas's 1880 entry will name his wife and surviving children, confirming the family unit. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Pennsylvania civil registration of deaths began 1 January 1906; Patrick Flynn died in 1908, so a state death certificate should exist. PA death certificates from this period include fields for father's name and mother's maiden name. The informant (likely a child or spouse) may not have known Patrick's parents accurately — making this derivative evidence — but a named parent on the certificate focuses all subsequent searches on a confirmed or near-confirmed target. FamilySearch has 'Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906-1963' indexed.

If Thomas Flynn died in Schuylkill County, his will or administration may name Patrick as a son — direct parentage evidence independent of census co-enumeration. Thomas was born ~1818 and most likely died between 1860 and 1900. FamilySearch has 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683) and a browse-only Schuylkill County probate image group (007936749, 1851-1930, 0% indexed). An indexed search in the collection should be tried first; if nothing surfaces, the browse-only film must be examined for Thomas Flynn entries.

A dedicated search for the marriage record of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required: it is the primary source likely to supply the mother's maiden name, which census and death records routinely omit. The marriage date, relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth, also confirms the couple as a unit before Patrick was born — direct corroboration of paternity. The Flynns were likely Catholic (Irish famine-era immigrants in a coal-mining county). FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936751 covers Schuylkill County church records 1842–1895 (0% indexed; includes German-language registers). County court marriage bonds should also be checked. This item is distinct from the baptism search (item 6): the target record, informant, and evidentiary yield are different.

A baptism record for Patrick Flynn would name his father and mother directly — the highest-quality parentage evidence available. Patrick was born ~1845 in Ireland but emigrated before 1850, so he may have been baptized in County Ireland before departure or in a Schuylkill County parish shortly after arrival. Search FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (1401638) and browse image group 007936751 (Schuylkill County church records 1842–1895). Any baptism entry for Patrick naming 'Thomas Flynn and [wife]' as parents would be direct, primary-quality evidence for q_001.

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — q_001: Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?

**Why a new plan?** pl_002 is completed. The proof summary is stuck at **probable** because: the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses are unsearched; Patrick's mother is completely unknown (no name, no maiden name, no tree entry); and Thomas Flynn's probate was only tried on FamilySearch. This plan closes those gaps.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Source | Target | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census 1870 | FamilySearch | Patrick Flynn (~25) and Thomas Flynn (~52), Schuylkill Co. | First census gap; Thomas's household names his wife — the only avenue to identify Patrick's mother |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census 1880 | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn (~62) and Patrick Flynn (~35), Schuylkill Co. | **1880 is the first census with an explicit relationship column** — a co-resident son is labeled "son," upgrading from indirect to direct evidence |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census 1900 | FamilySearch | Patrick Flynn (~55), Schuylkill Co. | Second census gap named in the proof summary; note the 1890 census is destroyed and cannot be searched |
| 4 | pli_010 | Church records — **parents' marriage** | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn's marriage to his wife, Schuylkill Co., 1835–1860 | **Required by parentage planning standards.** Supplies the mother's maiden name (absent from every record so far) and constitutes direct evidence of paternity by date; St. Patrick's, Pottsville (est. 1827) and PA county marriage bonds are the primary targets |
| 5 | pli_011 | Immigration / passenger lists | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn family arriving from Ireland, 1845–1850 | Family emigrated during the Famine years; a manifest listing Thomas + wife + infant Patrick is direct pre-America evidence of the parent-child relationship |
| 6 | pli_012 | Court — naturalization | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn, Schuylkill County courts, 1845–1875 | First/second naturalization papers may name his wife and Irish county of origin, narrowing any Ireland-side search |
| 7 | pli_013 | Probate | Ancestry | Thomas Flynn, Schuylkill Co., 1865–1905 | **Fallback for pli_006** (FamilySearch returned negative); Ancestry uses independent microfilm and indexing — a will naming Patrick as son would be the strongest possible evidence upgrade |

---

### Fallback relationship
If item 7 (pli_013, Ancestry probate) also returns nothing, the probate avenue is exhausted and research would pivot to land records and local-history biographical sketches.

---

### Scope
**7 searches.** Items 1–3 are free FamilySearch indexed searches and can be run quickly in sequence. Items 4–6 require more targeted repository navigation. Item 7 requires Ancestry access.

### What a positive result at step 2 would mean
Finding Thomas Flynn in the 1880 census with a co-resident labeled "son: Patrick" would upgrade the proof from *probable* to *proved* on its own — the explicit relationship column is the key piece the 1850 and 1860 censuses could never supply.

---

**Ready to start?** Say "search the 1870 census" and the search-records skill will execute pli_007 first.

entry.rationale: The 1870 census is the first major chronological gap identified in the proof summary. Patrick Flynn (~25) would likely still be in Schuylkill County; if still in Thomas Flynn's household (~52), a third census co-residence further supports the parent-child relationship. Critically, Thomas's 1870 household would record his wife's name — the only current avenue to identify Patrick's mother, who is completely absent from the tree and all assertions. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

The 1880 census is the first U.S. federal census to include an explicit relationship column, recording each person's relationship to the head of household ('son', 'daughter', 'wife', etc.). If Thomas Flynn (~62) is still living with any children, they will be labeled directly — supplying the kind of explicit parentage evidence the 1850 and 1860 censuses (which lack a relationship column) cannot provide. Even if Patrick (~35) heads his own household by 1880, his record confirms continued identity and may show him living near Thomas or with Thomas in his household. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick Flynn (~55) in the 1900 census fills the second major gap named in the proof summary. The 1900 census uniquely asks each woman the number of children born and number living — if Patrick's wife is enumerated, that data confirms family size. Thomas Flynn (~82) may also appear; his 1900 household would carry the explicit relationship column. Note: the 1890 census was almost entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire and cannot be searched for this period. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Per GPS planning standards for parentage questions, a dedicated search for the candidate parents' marriage to each other is required and must not be folded into a generic church-records item. Patrick's mother is entirely unknown — she appears in no assertion, no tree entry, and no searched record. Thomas Flynn's marriage record would: (a) confirm the couple as a unit; (b) supply the mother's maiden name, which U.S. census and death records routinely omit; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth, constitute direct parentage evidence capable of confirming or refuting the Thomas-Flynn-as-father hypothesis that currently rests only on indirect census co-residence and a secondary death-certificate informant. Schuylkill County's dominant Irish Catholic parishes — St. Patrick's, Pottsville (established 1827) and surrounding Mahanoy/Shenandoah area churches — recorded marriages from the 1820s forward. Here's the full plan:

---

## Research Plan pl_001 — q_001
**Who was the father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg (b. 20 Aug 1779, Stagstrup Sogn, Thisted Amt, Denmark)?**

The patronymic "Nielsen" implies a father named **Niels** — a lead, not yet evidence. No records have been searched. The Stagstrup kirkebog (vol. 004130616) covers 1763–1814 and is only 3% indexed; most searches below require browsing images.

---

| # | Record type | Jurisdiction / period | Repository | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Church — baptism | Stagstrup Sogn, 1779 | FamilySearch (col. 2078555, vol. 004130616) | Mikkel's 1779 baptism entry should name his father directly — the highest-probability single record |
| **2** | Church — parents' marriage | Stagstrup Sogn, 1755–1779 | FamilySearch (same vol.) | The marriage of Niels to Mikkel's mother distinguishes this Niels from other parish residents and confirms the parental couple (required for parentage questions) |
| **3** | Census 1787 | Stagstrup Sogn | FamilySearch (col. 1401625) / Dansk Demografisk Database | Mikkel (~8 yrs) was almost certainly in his father's household; the census lists relationships, providing independent corroboration |
| **4** | Military levy rolls (*lægdsruller*) | Thisted Amt, 1789–1814 | FamilySearch (col. 2513529, vol. 004748896) | Boys enrolled from early childhood **under the father's name** — the father's entry sits directly above Mikkel's; direct parentage evidence; session notations can also bracket the father's death |
| **5** | Probate (*skifteprotokol*) | Hassing Herred, Thisted Amt, 1760–1820 | FamilySearch (col. 2015318, vol. 004392710) | If the father Niels died, probate names all heirs by name and residence — direct father-son evidence and FAN cluster context |
| **6** *(fallback → #1)* | Church — baptism + marriage | Stagstrup Sogn, 1755–1790 | Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline (sa.dk) | Free browsable Thisted Amt church-book images; use if FamilySearch scan is illegible or incomplete |
| **7** *(fallback → #4)* | Military levy rolls | Thisted Amt, 1789–1814 | Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline (sa.dk) | Rigsarkivet's district-level organization may make the enrollment easier to locate if the FS volume is unclear |

**Fallback relationships:** If step 1 yields nothing → step 6. If step 4 yields nothing → step 7.

**Scope:** 5 primary searches + 2 fallbacks = up to 7 searches total.

---

Ready to start? Say **"execute the plan"** and I'll run step 1 — browsing the Stagstrup kirkebog for Mikkel's 1779 baptism entry.

entry.rationale: Stagstrup kirkebog (vol. 004130616, covering 1763–1814) is the single most direct source for this question. The 1779 baptism entry for Mikkel should name his father explicitly. The volume is only 3% indexed, so browsing the images is required. Collection 2078555 (Denmark, Church Books, 1484–1941).

The parents' marriage — father Niels to Mikkel's mother — predates the 1779 birth, likely in the 1760s–1770s. The same Stagstrup kirkebog (vol. 004130616) covers marriages. The marriage entry identifies both parties by name and origin, distinguishing this Niels from other Niels residents in the parish and confirming the parental couple. Required for parentage questions per BCG Standard 14.

Mikkel was ~8 years old in 1787 and almost certainly still living in his father's household. The 1787 Danish census lists all household members with their relationship to the head; finding the household of Niels with Mikkel listed as son corroborates the baptism-record identification independently and reveals the family's social context (occupation, farm). Fully searchable in FamilySearch collection 1401625; also available at Dansk Demografisk Database (ddd.dda.dk).

Danish lægdsruller enroll males from birth or early childhood under the father's name — the father's entry sits directly above the son's, making the roll direct evidence of paternity. Mikkel (b. 1779) would have been enrolled ca. 1789 at age ~10. Annual/triennial session notations can also bracket the father's death (e.g. 'gl. M. S.' → 'GBES' signals the father died between those sessions). FamilySearch collection 2513529; vol. 004748896 covers Vestervig/Thisted Amt districts 1789–1814 (0% indexed — browsing required).

If the father Niels died while Mikkel was a minor or thereafter, a probate (skifteprotokol) would name the widow and all living heirs — including Mikkel — with residences, providing direct father-son evidence and potentially naming siblings (FAN cluster). FamilySearch collection 2015318 (Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851) provides a searchable index; vol. 004392710 holds Hassing Herred originals (1690–1850). Confirm the correct herred for Stagstrup against the index before browsing.

Fallback for items 1 and 2. Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline (sa.dk/ao-soegesider) hosts free browsable images of Thisted Amt church books independent of the FamilySearch digitization. If the FamilySearch scan is difficult to read or the relevant pages are missing, the Rigsarkivet copy may offer better legibility or different image sets for the 1779 baptism and the parents' pre-1779 marriage.

Fallback for item 4. Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline (sa.dk/ao-soegesider) hosts free browsable lægdsruller organized by levying district. If the FamilySearch volume (004748896) is unclear or the Stagstrup district cannot be located within it, Rigsarkivet's district-level organization may make the enrollment easier to find.

Now writing the plan.Plan **pl_001** written — 6 items, all validated. Here's what was built and why.

---

## Research Plan — pl_001
**Question q_001:** When and where did Elijah Wilkins (b. c. 1814, Muhlenberg County, KY) die?

### ⚠️ Critical scope note
The question presupposes *death certificates*, but **Kentucky statewide certificates begin 1 January 1911 only**. County death registers cover 1874–1910 with gaps. Elijah's census trail ends at 1870 (age ~56), placing his probable death in the 1870s–1880s — a window where certificates may not exist. The plan leads with the county register index, then immediately backs it with probate, which is the primary death evidence for pre-1911 Kentucky deaths.

---

### Execution order

| # | Record type | Source | Notes |
|---|-------------|--------|-------|
| **1** | County death register index | **Kentucky, Deaths and Burials, 1843-1970** — FamilySearch (free) | Direct match for the question; covers Muhlenberg County 1874–1910 |
| **2** | Probate records | **Kentucky Probate Records, 1727-1990** — FamilySearch (browse-only, free) | Primary death evidence pre-1911; administrator bonds and estate settlements bracket the death date within months and name the family |
| **3** | County death register index | **Kentucky Death Records, 1852–1965** — Ancestry (paid) | *Fallback for item 1* — different transcription project, may cover gaps the FamilySearch index missed |
| **4** | Cemetery inscriptions | **Find a Grave** — Muhlenberg County (free) | Independent death date and burial place; especially valuable when civil registration is incomplete |
| **5** | Census / mortality schedule | **1880 US Mortality Schedule + 1880 Census** — FamilySearch (free) | Mortality schedule captures a June 1879–May 1880 death; 1880 population census confirms whether Elijah was still alive, sharpening the search window for all other items |
| **6** | Statewide death certificate | **Kentucky Deaths, 1911–1967** — FamilySearch (free) | Contingency: eliminates the small possibility he survived to age ~97+ |

**Fallback chain:** pli_003 (Ancestry) activates if pli_001 (FamilySearch county index) is empty.

---

**Estimated scope:** 6 searches, 4 free repositories + 1 paid (Ancestry) + 1 external site (Find a Grave).

**Suggested next step:** Start with item 1 — would you like me to execute the search against *Kentucky, Deaths and Burials, 1843–1970* on FamilySearch?

args.query: Kentucky death certificates vital records registration history genealogy

entry.rationale: Kentucky county death registers, where they survive, cover 1852-1859 and 1874-1910. 'Kentucky, Deaths and Burials, 1843-1970' (collection 1804888) indexes these county-level registers. Elijah's census trail ends at 1870 (age ~56), placing the likely death in the 1874-1910 window that county registers may cover for Muhlenberg County. This is the most direct, free digital index for the question's presupposed record type and the natural first search.

Kentucky statewide death certificates begin only in 1911; for deaths in the 1870s-1880s (Elijah's most probable window), probate records are the primary death evidence. 'Kentucky Probate Records, 1727-1990' (collection 1875188) provides browse-only images of county court wills, administrator bonds, estate settlements, and inventories. Muhlenberg County probate records survive well for the 1870s. An administrator's bond or estate settlement would bracket the death date within months, identify the county of death, and name heirs — confirming identity through family context.

Ancestry's 'Kentucky, U.S., Death Records, 1852-1965' (ancestry.com/search/collections/1222/) draws from county death registers and may index Muhlenberg County entries absent from or missed by the FamilySearch index. Searching this paid collection is the necessary fallback if item 1 yields nothing, given that the two indexes derive from different transcription projects and may have different coverage gaps.

Find a Grave (findagrave.com/cemetery/browse?location=Muhlenberg+County) hosts volunteer-transcribed cemetery inscriptions for Muhlenberg County. A headstone record would supply an independent death date and burial location, and the burial place typically confirms the county of death. Cemetery evidence is especially valuable when civil registration is incomplete, as in 1870s-1880s Kentucky, and provides a cross-check on any date found in the register or probate record.

Two searches: (a) the 1880 US Mortality Schedule (recording deaths June 1879-May 1880) would name Elijah if he died in that window; (b) the 1880 population census confirms whether he was alive in June 1880 — his presence narrows the death date to post-1880, his absence supports an earlier 1870s death. Both outcomes sharpen the search window for the death register and probate items, and serve as a FAN-cluster check via household members who might later appear in estate records.

Contingency search: if Elijah (b. c. 1814) survived to 1 January 1911 — the start of Kentucky statewide registration — a death certificate would be in 'Kentucky Deaths, 1911-1967' (collection 1417491). Survival to age ~97 is unlikely given the 1870 census endpoint, but the indexed search is fast and definitively eliminates the possibility. A positive result would be the clearest, most direct answer to the question as posed.

I have the full project picture. This is a **review** — `pl_002` (parentage question q_001) is active with one item currently `in_progress`. Let me check the FamilySearch collection for that step to make the recommendation actionable.Here is the full review of the active research plan for the parentage question.

---

## Research Plan Review — q_001: Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?

**Active plan:** `pl_002` · Created 2026-05-02 · 3 items

---

### ✅ Step 1 — `pli_004` · 1860 Census · FamilySearch · **Completed**

Searched the 1860 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County on FamilySearch. Found Patrick Flynn, age 15, in the household of Thomas Flynn (dwelling 112, family 119) — consistent with the 1850 co-enumeration. This is a second independent census placing Patrick in the Thomas Flynn household, strengthening the parent-child hypothesis (h_001).

---

### ✅ Step 2 — `pli_005` · Death Certificate · FamilySearch · **Completed**

Located Patrick Flynn's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate (no. 4521). The informant, son-in-law James Brown, named **Thomas Flynn** as Patrick's father. This is **direct evidence** of the parentage relationship, though classified as **secondary information** (Brown was not a firsthand witness to Patrick's birth). The death cert also introduced a birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania), resolved in favor of Ireland via the two earlier census records — conflict `c_001` is resolved.

---

### 🔄 Step 3 — `pli_006` · Probate Records · Schuylkill County, 1870–1890 · FamilySearch · **In Progress — this is the next step**

**Goal:** Locate Thomas Flynn's will or estate administration in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. A will naming Patrick as a son would be **direct evidence of parentage from a primary informant** (Thomas Flynn himself), which would upgrade the conclusion from *Probable* to *Proved* and address the proof summary's own stated gap.

**Collection confirmed:** [Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683) — collection ID `1417683`, 2.3 million images on FamilySearch.

---

## Research Plan pl_002 — 8 items

**Starting-point note (BCG Standard 11):** The only documented evidence for q_001 so far is Patrick's co-enumeration in Thomas Flynn's 1850 household — indirect evidence that supports but does not prove parentage. The mother is entirely unknown. The proof tier is "possible." Four sources already appear in the tree (S2, S3, S4, plus the 1850 census) but only the 1850 census has been formally extracted for q_001 assertions; the rest are placeholders to be worked through this plan.

---

### Step 1 — Pennsylvania Death Certificate, Patrick Flynn, 1908 `pli_004`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (collection 2255317 — free)  
**Period:** 1906–1968  
Pennsylvania began statewide death registration in 1906; Patrick died 1908-03-12, squarely within scope. Death certificates from this era include the deceased's **parents' names and birthplaces** — if the informant knew them, this single record resolves q_001 with direct evidence. This is the highest-priority item in the plan. The tree references S3 but no parentage assertions for q_001 have been extracted.

---

### Step 2 — 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County `pli_005`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (free)  
Patrick would be ~15, likely still in Thomas's household. Co-enumeration across two consecutive censuses (1850 + 1860) substantially strengthens the indirect parentage evidence beyond a single data point. Builds the multi-record co-residence pattern before the 1880 relationship column is available. The tree references S2 but no q_001 assertions have been extracted.

---

### Step 3 — 1880 U.S. Census, Pennsylvania `pli_006`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (free)  
The **first census with an explicit "relationship to head" column.** Patrick (~35) may still reside with or near Thomas (~62). A stated relationship of "son" in this census would be the strongest indirect census evidence obtainable. Pennsylvania-wide scope accounts for possible relocation within the state.

---

### Step 4 — Schuylkill County Probate Records, Thomas Flynn `pli_007`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (collection 1417683 indexed + image group 007936749 browse-only)  
Thomas (born ~1818) likely died 1870–1905. A **will naming Patrick as a son or heir** is direct evidence of the father-son relationship. The indexed FamilySearch collection should be checked first; if nothing surfaces, the 0%-indexed browse-only volume (007936749, covering 1851–1930) must be browsed decade by decade. Tree source S4 exists but no q_001 assertions extracted.

---

### Step 5 — Parents' Marriage Record: Thomas Flynn + Wife `pli_008`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (Irish Catholic parish registers; PA church collection 1401638)  
**Period:** 1835–1850 | **Jurisdiction:** Ireland; Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania  
Required for every parentage plan per GPS standards. Thomas (b. ~1818 Ireland) married before Patrick's ~1845 birth — almost certainly in Ireland ca. 1835–1844. This record will: **(a)** confirm Thomas and his wife as a unit; **(b)** supply the **mother's maiden name** (absent from every census and death record); and **(c)** provide direct paternity evidence by its date relative to Patrick's birth. The Irish county is unknown at present — findings from the death certificate (Step 1) and naturalization records (Step 7) may narrow the search to a specific county before this item is executed.

---

### Step 6 — Catholic Church Records, Diocese of Scranton / Schuylkill County Parishes `pli_009`
**Repository:** Other (Diocese of Scranton Archives; FamilySearch image group 007936751)  
**Period:** 1842–1895  
The Flynn family was almost certainly **Irish Catholic** — the dominant religious community in Schuylkill County's anthracite coal region. A baptism record for Patrick in a Pottsville, Minersville, or Shenandoah parish would name his father (and possibly mother) as **direct parentage evidence**. Burial or death records for Thomas Flynn establish the date range for the probate search. FamilySearch's browse-only volume 007936751 covers these records at 0% indexed; the Diocese of Scranton Archives holds additional original registers. Key parishes: St. Patrick's Pottsville (est. 1827), St. Joseph's Minersville, Annunciation Shenandoah.

---

### Step 7 — Schuylkill County Naturalization Records, Thomas Flynn `pli_010`
**Repository:** FamilySearch (image group 007936750, browse-only, 0% indexed)  
**Period:** 1849–1915  
**FAN-directed item.** Thomas Flynn would have naturalized ~1849–1870. Early declarations sometimes list the declarant's wife and children — collateral evidence of family composition. More importantly, naturalization papers often name the **Irish county of origin**, which would enable the Irish marriage search (Step 5) to be scoped to a specific county and townland — a significant unlock for that otherwise open-ended search.

---

### Step 8 — Ancestry: Pennsylvania Death Certificates (fallback for Step 1) `pli_011`
**Repository:** Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_004*  
**Period:** 1906–1963  
Execute only if the FamilySearch death certificate search (Step 1) yields no result or an unreadable image. Ancestry carries an independent index with separate transcriptions of the same underlying records. Two access paths, one underlying original.

---

Search FamilySearch Irish Catholic parish registers (county unknown — the death certificate and naturalization searches may supply the townland/county, making this item more targeted after those results). Also search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (collection 1401638) as a secondary target in case the couple married in Pennsylvania shortly before the 1850 census.

entry.jurisdiction: Diocese of Scranton, Pennsylvania (Schuylkill County parishes)
entry.rationale: The Flynn family was almost certainly Irish Catholic — the predominant religious affiliation of mid-19th-century Irish immigrants to Schuylkill County's anthracite coal region. The Diocese of Scranton (established 1868; before that, Diocese of Philadelphia) holds sacramental registers for Schuylkill County parishes. A baptism record for Patrick would name his father (and mother), constituting direct parentage evidence. Burial or marriage records for Thomas Flynn would establish dates for the probate and marriage searches. FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936751 covers Schuylkill County church records 1842–1895 at 0% indexed. Primary Catholic parishes to check: St. Patrick's Pottsville (est. 1827), St. Joseph's Minersville, Annunciation Shenandoah. The Diocese of Scranton Archives also holds original registers not yet digitized.

As an Irish immigrant, Thomas Flynn would have naturalized, most likely between 1849 and 1870. Early naturalization declarations (pre-1906) sometimes list the declarant's wife's name and children, providing collateral evidence of family composition and confirming the family unit. FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936750 covers Schuylkill County naturalization records 1849–1915 at 0% indexed — this is a FAN-directed item targeting Thomas Flynn's own records rather than Patrick's. A successful find may also identify the Irish county of origin, which is currently unknown, enabling the Irish marriage search (item 5) to be scoped to a specific county and townland.

Fallback for the FamilySearch death certificate search (pli_004). Ancestry maintains an independent index of Pennsylvania death certificates 1906–1963 with separate transcriptions. If FamilySearch returns no result or the image quality is insufficient to read the parents' fields, Ancestry's index provides a second access path to the same underlying records. Search only if pli_004 is unsuccessful or inconclusive.

The plan correctly references the FamilySearch collections covering Pennsylvania statewide, with Schuylkill County as the search focus. No anachronistic jurisdictions or pre-statehood errors. The skill accurately notes that Pennsylvania had no civil death registration before 1906, making church/probate records appropriate for this window.

dimensions.name: Plan mode and lifecycle
dimensions.rationale: Correct supersede mode applied. The skill recognized that new information (Thomas Flynn death ca. 1865) invalidated pl_002's active 1870-1890 probate window. It marked pl_002.status='superseded' and created a new pl_003 with status='active'. Prior plan items (pli_004, pli_005 from completed pl_002) remain untouched in audit trail. Exactly one active plan (pl_003) now exists for q_001. No duplicate plans created alongside pl_002, no in-place edits to existing items. Lifecycle fully correct per test expectations.
